summary:
The Dungan Revolt (1862–77) was a series of uprisings by Muslim populations — primarily the Hui (Chinese Muslims) and later Turkic peoples of Xinjiang — against [[event:2337|Qing dynasty]] rule in northwestern China. The conflicts were among the deadliest of the catastrophic mid-century rebellions that shook the Qing empire, with death tolls estimated in the millions from violence, famine, and displacement.

The revolts had multiple, overlapping causes: religious tension between Muslim communities and Han Chinese neighbors, local grievances over taxation and land, and the broader instability created by the [[event:2543|Taiping Rebellion]] which had drawn Qing military resources away from the northwest. The northwestern revolts centered in Shaanxi and Gansu provinces, while a separate and partly connected uprising under Yakub Beg established an independent state in Xinjiang (Eastern Turkestan) from 1865 to 1877. Yakub Beg's regime attracted diplomatic attention from Britain and Russia, both of which had strategic interests in Central Asia.

The suppression was led by Zuo Zongtang, one of the most capable Qing commanders, who conducted a methodical campaign westward from 1867. Zuo rebuilt supply lines, constructed roads, and moved his forces steadily across the devastated northwest. By 1873 the Gansu revolt was suppressed; in 1877 Zuo's forces reconquered Xinjiang, ending Yakub Beg's state. The Qing subsequently incorporated Xinjiang formally as a province in 1884, the first time it had been administered as a regular part of China rather than a military frontier territory.
